theorem_statement:
Finite-time blow-up from a virial identity for focusing mass-critical NLS.

Let \(u(t,x)\) be a sufficiently regular solution on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) with finite variance. Assume:

1. mass is conserved:

\[
\|u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}=\|u_0\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}=:M>0,
\]

2. energy is conserved and negative:

\[
E(u(t))=E(u_0)<0,
\]

3. the variance

\[
V(t)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx
\]

satisfies

\[
V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0,
\]

4. for every sufficiently regular \(f\),

\[
\|f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}^2
\le
\frac{2}{d}\,\|x f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\,\|\nabla f\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}.
\]

Then there exists \(T<\infty\) such that

\[
\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty
\quad\text{as } t\uparrow T.
\]

nodes.title: Finite-time blow-up from the virial identity
nodes.informal_statement:
There exists \(T<\infty\) such that
\[
\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty
\quad\text{as } t\uparrow T.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Let \(T>0\) be the finite time from the variance-vanishing claim. The weighted estimate gives
\[
M^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}
\]
for every time of existence. Here \(M>0\) is fixed, while \(V(t)\to 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\). Therefore the right-hand side can stay at least \(M^2\) only if
\[
\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\to\infty
\quad\text{as } t\uparrow T.
\]
Thus the solution blows up in finite positive time.

nodes.title: The variance reaches zero in finite positive time
nodes.informal_statement:
There exists a finite time \(T>0\) such that
\[
V(T)=0,
\]
and hence
\[
V(t)\to 0
\quad\text{as } t\uparrow T.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Since \(E(u_0)<0\), the virial identity gives \(V''(t)=16E(u_0)<0\), so \(V\) is strictly concave. Integrating twice yields
\[
V(t)=8E(u_0)t^2+at+b
\]
for some constants \(a,b\). Because \(E(u_0)<0\), the coefficient of \(t^2\) is negative, so \(V(t)\to -\infty\) as \(t\to +\infty\). But by definition
\[
V(t)=\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}|x|^2|u(t,x)|^2\,dx\ge 0
\]
for every time on which the solution exists. Therefore \(V\) cannot stay positive for all \(t\ge 0\). By continuity there exists a finite \(T>0\) with \(V(T)=0\), equivalently \(V(t)\to 0\) as \(t\uparrow T\).

nodes.title: Mass and the weighted inequality force a lower bound involving \(\sqrt{V(t)}\)
nodes.informal_statement:
For every time of existence,
\[
M^2\le \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{V(t)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Apply the weighted inequality with \(f=u(t)\):
\[
\|u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}^2
\le
\frac{2}{d}\,\|x u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}.
\]
Mass conservation gives \(\|u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}=M\), hence
\[
M^2\le
\frac{2}{d}\,\|x u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}\,\|\nabla u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}.
\]
By the definition of the variance,
\[
\|x u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}^2=V(t),
\]
so \(\|x u(t)\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}=\sqrt{V(t)}\). Substituting gives the claimed inequality.
nodes.formalization_rationale: Most conservative local island: it is a direct one-step derivation from the assumed weighted inequality, mass conservation, and the definition of variance. It is technically self-contained, avoids global blow-up/existence machinery, and should formalize with low dependency overhead compared with the finite-time root argument or the concavity-to-first-zero claim.

edges.label: depends on
edges.explanation: The blow-up time is obtained from the finite time at which the variance tends to zero.

edges.label: depends on
edges.explanation: The weighted inequality turns small variance and positive mass into divergence of the gradient norm.
